skill.name: ai-tools
skill.description: Reference guide for DBX Studio AI chat tool definitions, execution flow, provider mappings, and system prompt locations.
skill.summary: Map DBX Studio AI tools, providers, and implementation files for maintenance work.
skill.author: Dbxstudio

security_audit.summary: The static findings are false positives from Markdown code spans, function names, and ordinary words such as description, metadata, and SYSTEM_PROMPT_WITH_TOOLS. The file is documentation only and contains no executable shell commands, Ruby backtick execution, prompt injection attempt, weak cryptographic operation, or Windows SAM database access.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Spans Flagged as Commands
low_findings.description: The reported external command findings are Markdown backticks around tool names, parameter names, provider files, and function names. They document DBX Studio AI tooling and do not execute commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious tokens are inside Markdown tables, inline code spans, and a fenced TypeScript reference block. No shell interpreter, Ruby code, subprocess call, or executable script is present.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Security Keywords Matched in Documentation
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography and Windows SAM detections come from substrings in documentation terms, including description, metadata, and SYSTEM_PROMPT_WITH_TOOLS. No cryptographic API or protected Windows file path is referenced.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review shows ordinary prose and identifier names rather than DES, MD5, SAM database paths, credential access, or related implementation code.

content.user_title: Document DBX Studio AI Tool Integrations
content.value_statement: DBX Studio AI tool maintenance requires quick knowledge of tool definitions, executors, providers, and prompts. This skill gives developers a compact reference for finding and updating those integration points.
actual_capabilities: Identifies the source file for DBX Studio AI tool definitions.
Identifies the source file for DBX Studio AI tool execution logic.
Lists available AI chat tools and their required parameters.
Maps AI provider service IDs to implementation files and functions.
Summarizes tool format conversions for Anthropic, OpenAI, and Bedrock.
Points developers to system prompt locations used by the AI chat flow.
limitations: It is a reference document, not an executable tool.
It does not inspect the current repository state automatically.
It does not validate whether linked source files still match the reference.
It focuses only on DBX Studio AI chat tooling.

use_cases.title: Add a DBX Studio AI Tool
use_cases.description: Use the reference to find where to add a new AI tool definition and executor handler.

use_cases.title: Debug Provider Tool Formatting
use_cases.description: Use the provider map to trace how DBX Studio converts tool definitions for each AI provider.

use_cases.title: Review AI Chat Prompt Locations
use_cases.description: Use the prompt location notes to find the system prompts that shape DBX Studio tool behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Find Tool Files
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-tools reference to show me where DBX Studio defines AI tools and where it executes them.

prompt_templates.title: Add One Tool
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-tools reference to outline the DBX Studio files and steps needed to add a new AI tool.

prompt_templates.title: Trace Provider Support
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-tools reference to trace how DBX Studio exposes tools to OpenAI, Anthropic, and Bedrock.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Tool Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the ai-tools reference to review DBX Studio AI tool flow from schema definition through provider formatting and execution.

output_examples.input: Where do I add a new DBX Studio AI chat tool?
output_examples.output: Add the tool to the AI tool definitions file, then add execution logic in the tool executor switch. The provider adapters pick up the shared definitions.

output_examples.input: Which providers need tool format conversion?
output_examples.output: The reference names Anthropic, OpenAI, and Bedrock conversions. It also lists provider entries for Qwen, Ollama, Gemini, Groq, and DBX Agent.

output_examples.input: Where should I look for system prompts?
output_examples.output: Check the main streaming system prompt location and the oRPC chat system prompt constant listed in the reference.

best_practices: Verify referenced file paths before changing production AI tool behavior.
Update tool definitions and executor logic together to prevent unsupported tool calls.
Review provider conversions after changing input schemas or parameter names.
anti_patterns: Do not add a tool definition without matching execution logic.
Do not assume every provider consumes the same tool format.
Do not change system prompts without reviewing tool behavior and provider responses.

faq.question: Does this skill execute DBX Studio tools?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation reference for developers who maintain DBX Studio AI tool integrations.

faq.question: Which AI providers are covered?
faq.answer: The reference lists AWS Bedrock, OpenAI, Anthropic, Qwen, Ollama, Gemini, Groq, and DBX Agent.

faq.question: Does it include the tool executor location?
faq.answer: Yes. It points to the DBX Studio file that contains tool execution logic.

faq.question: Can it help add a new tool?
faq.answer: Yes. It lists the main steps for adding a definition, executor handler, and provider support.

faq.question: Does it validate SQL safety?
faq.answer: No. It only documents that DBX Studio has query-related tools and where their implementation lives.

faq.question: Is this skill user-invocable?
faq.answer: No. The skill metadata marks it as not user-invocable.
